FATHER MATTHIEW RUSSELL.

An interesting personality has been removed from Dublin literary
" circles by the death of Father Matthew Russell, S.J., whioch occurred
at a private hospital on 12th September. The younger brother
b of Lord Russell, of Killowen, e was born in the house now mumbered
£ 50, Queen Street, Newry, on 13th July, 1834, and his early years were
pasced at Seafield, Killowen, on the shores of Carlingford Lough, a
~ spot to which he always referred affectionately, and frequently return-
el. At the conclusion of his home education he was sent to St. Vin-
:  cen 's, Qustleknock, to prepare for Maynooth and holy orders.  He
sums up his career in some curious autobiographical verses written two
years ago thus:—
“Im my Ilife the year 11,
Took my father home to heaven.
When my years were 22,
A Maynooth subdeacon flew
To a safe retreat, to be
Jesull priest at 33.
(My * Three-Three’’ was '67
When my mother went to heaven,
But I was, for sake of her,
Priested three years earlier.)
‘ X mmanuel’ at 44
Was tho first of books galore,
Xavier’s holy Dublin hive
Wias my homo at 55.”
There the remainder of his long hfe was spent as an éxem-
‘p]my pricet,  That life, so much of it as did not belong to religiom,
§ -Wa‘ﬂ devoted to literature, and many graceful and touching poems—

i‘ﬁ];lgmu,g and scoular—proceeded from his pen for mearly sixty yeans,

111& first appearing in ‘ Duffy’s Fireside Magazine”’ for December,
31853 Though fiftcen years were to clapse hefore he again appeared In

print, yet it will be secn that hie mas a prolific writer and editop

of
many works. DBut it is as the founder and editor of * The Irish

Mouthly > that he deserves the thanks and grateful remembrance of
all interested in Anglo-Irish literature.

In this, always charming,
magazine, which he started in'1873, he oathered all the carlier efforts
of the Irish men and women writers whose rames are now householq
words, and gave that first start and judicious prailse so encouraging
and so neccessary. Oscar Wilde, W. B. Yeats, Hilaire Belloe, “ M. |
I'rancis,” Katherite Tynan, Rose Kavanagh, Dora Sigerson Shorter,
and hests of others could bear testimony to his editorial kindness and
acumen, whilst the contributions of more tried writers like Rosa Mul-
holland (Lady Gilbert), Daniel Crilly, etc., and many selections from
the valuable correspondence of his uncle, Rev. Dr. Ruesell, of May-

nooth, lent a ¢harm and a value to its pages; not to memtion his own

T o T . T R . o N R L Y

articles which, dealing with many and various subjects, graced nearly
every 1ssue. He contributed much to other organs, and he placed his
wide knowledge of Irish history at the disposal of his great brother
when preparing his memorable speech before the Parnell Commission.
That brother, on his elevation to the peenage, wrote to him, ‘ You
have always been to me kind, generous and indulgent,” and no fitter
epitaph could be selected for FFather Matthew Russell.

PRINTING IN CLONMEL 1801-25.

At p. 75, vol. 1u., I gave a facsimile of an 18th century title-page

' of a Clonmel printed book. With this issue I present another, which
3 illustrates the advance of printing in the town during the interval
1786-1811. The succeeding list of printers and printing ix a lengthy

one, and contains some very interesting items. IL. R. McC. DIX.

‘ 1802 The Clonmel Herald. Vol. I., No. 9. Wednesday, 30th June.

Burke (1907), p. 859.)

MORE ABOUT AUTOGRAPHS.

Messrs. Maggs, of the Strand, have again issued a fine catalogue
(293) of Autograph Letters of distinguished literary men, which, even
to read is a treat to those whom the res angusta domi prevent enterimg
into competition for their possession with American millionaires. Not
e few being by Imsh writers, or on Irish matters, will be of espeocial
interest to our readers, containing, as they do, some intimate touches

not to be found in ordinary biographies. Thus, Aubrey De Vere, writ-
ing on August 4th, 1889, tells a oo-r.re&po.nde-nt, “I am engaged to the

Tennysons at Hazelmere to-morrow.’”’” Prof. Dowden, under date of

. .8rd February, 1884, tells a lady, ‘‘ My present occupation is working
 towards a life of Shelley. Sir Peroy and Lady Shelley have given me

ffffff

access to all the papers in their possession.” A long letter dated

August, ’68, from John Leech, the ‘‘ Punch ’ artisy, whose father Was

o, e e
i =

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 47

iaqn Irishman, a man of fine culture, a profound Shakesperean scholar,
and a thorou2h gantleman,” gives his first impressions of the old land
which he visited with Dean Hole, the outcome being ‘‘ A Little Tour
in Ireland,” 1859. He says, ‘‘ There is beauty amongst the women of
the very highest order. Limerick, for example, is justly celebrated for
the comelinesw of its lassies—nearly every girl I saw there was pretty—
and in other parts they ‘are as beautiful and as graceful as the most
fastidious antist could desire. 1 have brought over for you a genuine
shilaleh made out of black thorn, cut in the Dark Moon, whatever that
means. Apropos of Shilalehs, I went to Donnybrook TFair, but I
confers I did not meet with any of the ready wit so much talked about.’’
There 1s a letter from Tom Moore to Lady Morgan, December 22nd,
1830, which contains one or two biographical facts not hitherto noted.
“I wag onrce offered, at the rate of £100 a month, to conduct the Times
for a certain pericd, and at another time had a proposal from Constable
to edit the Edinburgh Review at £1,000 a_ year, but neither tempted
me.” (This is hardly consistent with an entry in the ‘ Diary?”’ for
June, 1823. Moore was quite willing, but Jeffrey couldn’t decently be
got rid of.) ‘‘People express a little alarm about my * Life and Death
of Lord Edward,” and I get hinte from all sides that it would be prudent
to defer its publication, hut I shall not mind them.’”” Justin McCarthy
informed Mrs. Forbes, on 26th February, 1899, “I have so much work
ahead of me of a less fascinating order than fiation -that I don’t know
mwhen I ghall be able to attempt another novel.”” There is a remarkable
letter from Lord Orrery defending his * Remarks on Swift,” whose
appearance had created a storm of criticism. He writes to Dr. Barry,
on 10th December, 1751, ‘‘ In England they think I have said too little.
In Treland théy think I have said too much. . . . I am surprised
that Delaney should think I had treated the Dean cruelly, when he
has often vented to me his spleen against the Dean, sometimes in the
heat of prose, sometimes in the heat of verse, but always in a heat. I
haye spoken the truth, but I have not spoken the whole truth ; so tender
have I been of my friend the Dean. But my great offence is that I
h‘-ave hindered some low flattering scribblers from screening and sell-
ing their nonsense under the Dean’s name. . . . The sale of the

- “hook goes on with an amazing rapidity. A third edition comes out
J"hlﬂ day, and a fourth is prefpm'mg My bookseller, one of the greatest |
-L'ln town, protests that he never kmew any book sell so fast.”” Next
»Gﬂmee an mrbere@bm g letter from Onl)t Ma.y:ne Reid, setting forth in
i h Ho wishes to publish a

48 THE IRISH BOOK LOVIIR.

novel to ““assail the Toryism of to-day (1878) through that of the
days of Carolus Rex, with their scurvy rabble of so-called cavaliers»
Here is a plagyful note from Lord John Russell to Moore. “I 4y
actually going to Ireland, which I was never sure of before. Now j
you could manage to join me in weeing Killarney, I think we migh
spend a fortnnght together very pleasantly. You may be as patriotig
as you pleaso about the ‘first flower e¢f the earth and first gem of the
sea ' ; indeed, your heing a rebel may somewhat atone for my being 4
Cabinet Mimister.”” Lastly comes ‘‘a short note concerning some
books”’ from I. M. the Duke of Wellington. Fancy the ‘‘ Iron Duke”
in the role of an Irish book lover!

DAVID COLHOUN.

Through the vigilance of the Editor of [.B.L. I recently acquired a
very rare little 16mo. volumo entitled ‘‘ Poems on Several Occasions, by
David Colhoun, or the Shepherd of Mary Grey,”” vol. I. 1 already pos-
sessed vol. II. (Strabane: Carroll and TFoster, 1812.) Vol. 1. was printed
at Strabane in 1810 by Jobn Moore, a name new to me as a Strabane
printer, and hitherto unknowm to Mr. Dix, also. The little book con-
sists of xiv. + 62 pp. A shert life ¢f the author, by ‘‘a gentleman—his
friend,”’ is prefixed, from which it appears that he was born at New-
townstewart, Co. Tyronre, on 18th March, 1747 (0.S.). His father, who
claimed descent from Sir James Coulhoun, of Luss, in Scotland, was in
reduced circumstancee, but David, at the age of 14, prevailed on ham to
place him at a Latin wchool. When his father could no longer afford
the expense of this tuition, David himself established an English school
and earned ecufficient to enable him to resumeo his classical studies.
The youth’s perseverance raised him up friends among the gentlemen
of tho neighbourhood, one of whom offered to procure him a place 1
the Revenue, which he declined, and another proposed to pay a fee of
£50 to have him apprenticed to the trade of watchmaking. David, how-
ever, preferred to go his own way, and supported himself by making
musical instruments, mostly violing, keeping up, at the same time, his
study of Latin and Greek. He first attempted writing verse at the age
of 15. In hig twenty-fourth year he married. Tho consequent desor-
tion of hig friendg ‘‘drove him,” writes his biographer, ¢ from the
flowery paths of Parnassus to work out a scanty independence from the

stubborn and sterile bosom of a mountain farm.” (Tho mountain wasé
Mary Grey.) At the end of 14 yocars hiw wife died. Shortly after ho
married again, but his second wifo died in less than a year. In this

THIE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 49)

dark hour the Rev. Dr. Wilson, Rector of the Parish of Ardstraw, 1786-
1799, took the poet into his famaly, and offercd to prepare him for
college. But Colhoun declined, believing hig memory to be impaired by
the sorrows he had endured. Dr. Wilson, however, had him appointed
clerk of the parih, and he again married. About this time Lord Mount-
joy appointed him overseer of the manor of Newtownstewart.

Colhoun’s poems arc far above the average of country rhymsters’
offorts at this period.

Dr. Fitzgerald, rector of Ardstraw, 1806-1819, refers to him in an
account of the parish published in Shaw Mason’s Parochial Survey, vol.
[. (Dublin, 1814), as ‘‘an antiquary and a poet, and what is of more

~ consequence to him, clerk of the parish’!
A. A. CAMPBELL.

IRISH LITERARY SOCIETY.

On May 18th at a History Night a paper by Mr. H. Thorp on
“Lecky’’ was recad. The lecturer began by deploring the neglect of
Irigh history in Ireland. It had been little studied, and seldom well-
written English histories had, for the mest part, either neglected
Irish higtory, or treated it with the strongest prejudice.  Irish his-
torians had too often treated it in a partisan spirit. Lecky stood al-
mosd alone in that, while he always wrote as an Irishman and a pat-
riot ; he was also a historian of the highest type with allthe philosophic
judgment and the impartiality which the true historian should possess.

The lecturer then spoke of Lecky’s ancestry, of his birth in the
county Dublin, of hiw bringing-up and of his schooldays. He considered
that Lecky’s time in Trinity was especially influential in forming his
carcer, for Lecky was a thorough Trinity man. In the College His-
't@r;ica-l Society he played a great part, and he had high oratorical quali-
tieé, which nover reached their full development. While still a Trimty
student he produced a work which ought to be dear to every Irishman—
hig « Leaders of Public Opinion in Ireland.”

The Jlecturer then tried to summarise Lecky’s bmilliant treatment of
Swift, Flood, Grattan and O’Connell. The sketches of Swift and Grat-
tan were, perhaps, the most successful; with Grattan he had moro
Sympathy than with any of the others.

Afiter dealing shortly with Lecky’s importand works on the histony

of philogophy and with his ¢ History of England in the 18th Contury,”’
the leoturer passed to tho  History of Ireland in the 18th Century.””

The lecturer then gave an account of Lecky’s declining years and =

death, and acknowledized his debt to the excellent biography of the his.

and vindicator of his country’s fame.

GOSSLP.

The D.N.B., in dealing with Laetitia Pilkington, authoress, adven-
turesp and friend of Swifé, says her divorced husband, Matthew, a poet
and penniless Irish parson, ‘‘ must be carefully distinguished from the
author of the ‘Dictionary of Painters,”’’ and Thomas Seccombe, the
writer, gives memoirs of both. This gtatement has been copied into
many other works of reference. Now, by zealous research in the Public
Record Office, Dublin, necessitated by his new edition of Swift, Dr.
Elrington Ball has discovered they were one and the same person, and
gives a trarvecript of his will in ‘““ Notes and Queries ”’ for 27th July.

Mr. J. F. Meehan, of Ggy Street, Bath, one of the most enterpris-
ing West of Iingland hocksellers, also possceses the pen of a ready
writer, as his works on the city of his adoption, its literary and his-
torical associations, prove. ‘His father, the late Mr. Bartholomew
Meehan, the founder of the firmm, was born within sound of eweet
Shandon bellg in 1816, and after a roving, restless career was told ‘‘to
go to Bath,”” which he did in '67. Many stories still circulate of his
ready Irishh wit.  Once he marrowly escaped drowning.  *“‘ You wero
nearly gone that time,”” said a sympathetic onlooker. ‘‘No, no, not
the slightest danger.” ‘‘How’s that??” “I’'m a Cork man!?”

The O’Meehan family had been rettled for centuries in Co. Leitrim,

-t ey Fo
'y L ¥ . P

and had in their possession up to 1859 a silver casket containing the =

Gospel of St. Molaise of Devenish, a magnificent specimen of early
Irish, workmanship, now preserved in R.I.A. 8o the love of books 18
evidently hereditary.

THI IRISH BOOK LOVER. 51

Mr. Filson Young, one of the most fertile and brilliant of present-
day journalists, Intends issuing in volume form a selection from the

" which he has been contributing to the * Saturday Review

«mddles
during the past year. I hope he will include his more recent one
describing his boyish recollections of his holidays in the home of his
forbears. 1 have read nothing in recent journalism so vivid, eéparkling
aund true to hifo as his description of the drive in the long car from
Belfagt to Portaferry.

The mest notable contribution (in my view) to the September maga-
zines 1s an article in “* The Nineteenth Century,” by J. B. Williams,
entitled ““ I'resh Light cn Cromwell at Drogheda.” 1In it the writer
proves conclusively from contemporary sources that Cromwell, in his
famous dispatch to Speaker Lenthall, hitherto regarded as the most
reliable account of the wtorm and sack of the town, suppressed many
faots of the massacre, altered dates, minimized the slaughter and
passed over the treachery towards the surrendered soldiery. He also
shows that Carlyle altered words in original documents and omitted
damnatory evidence:; whilst S. R. Gardiner, hitherto regarded as a
most accurate and impartial historian, also toned down statements
glozed over one ‘‘ terrible letter,” and quoted only portions of docu-
ments condemnatory of Cromwell’s conduct.

Personally, I do not place implicit faith in the various news-shects
of the period—and T have gone through all the Thomasson Tracts re-
lating to Ireland. They contain many errors, palpablé on the face of
them, and at the best much exaggeration. They remind one, in many
Cases, of the ‘‘extra special ”’ catch-penny editions of our newspapers
In war-time, But I must admit that Mr. Williams does not depend
solely upon these sources of information, and that he handles his
Mmaterials with, judicial skill and apparent fairness that assures a con-
viction of Crommwell’s falsity.

Watervorp, 1912, We are pleased to see that a second edition of

JoHN WESLEY AND IrELAND. By J. G. Swift MacNeill, K.C., M.P.
This 1s Uhe substance of an address delivered by Prof. MacNeill in
London recently, and i1s mainly derived from Wesley’'s own jJournals.
In addition he shows the close relationship betiween the founder of
Methodism and the hero of Waterloo. Altogether an interesting and
timely little work. ‘

Wirnniay VinceNnt WALLAGE: & memowr by W. H. Grattan Flood.
(Waterford News.) There were two qualifications essential for writing
the lifs of a composer such as Vincent Wiallace—a thorough know-
lodge of music, and the possersion of a practised and sympathetic pen.
These two are cembined in tihe person of the author, and the result i
a charming account of the roving career of the composer and a masterly
pppreciation of his genius. This little sork 3a published at the low
price of sixpence, and the profits are to be devoted to a fund now being
raised for the purpose of erecting a monmument to Wallace in his natal

aty, a patriotic idea which, emanating from Mr. Edmund Downey, has

secured the support of such widely divergent public men am Lord

Roberts and Mr. John Redmond. A representative committee has been

- —

formed, amd Mr. Downqy, as secretary, will gladly acknowledge any

sulscription,

The ‘‘ Dublin Journal ’’ of 21st July, 1818, contains the following
announcement of the marriage of the Roundwood printer:—‘ In the
parish church of Derrafossory (Derrylossary?), by the Rev. L. W.
Hepenstal, G. B. P. Bull, printer, to Dorothea, second daughter of the
late B. Burland, T'eq., of Rockfield, County Wicklow.’ The latest ex-
tant item of Roundwood printing is dated 1820, so 1t is probable that
the printer left the Wicklow village (for Dublin) in that year. At any
rate, his name does not appear in the 1821 Census for that district,
though Synge’s school with which the press may have been connected
wag etill in existence. SEAMUS UA CASAIDE.

G. P. Bull, printer, resided at No. 3, Redmond’s Hill, in 1823, and
his name at that address is given in Pigot’s Directory for 1824. Prob-

~ably he was the Roundwood printer. Mr. Maffet says that he cannot

trace Edward Bull, printer, 15, Richmond Street, South, after the year
1837, hut T find his name given in 1844 as proprietor of the ‘‘ Warder
and Statesman,” 59, Ileet Street, residing at 37, Mount Pleasant
Avenue, Rathmines. At least I assume that the Edward Bull of 1837

18 the same as Tdward Bull of 1844. T have a good working set of

Toe RosaxNa Press. When in Wicklow churchyard, in the begin-
ning of June, for the purpose of taking inscriptions for the journal ¢f

the ‘‘ Association for the Preservation of the Memorials of the Dead,”

1 had a conversation with Mr. IFrank McPhail, J.P., proprietor of the

“ Wicklow: News-Letter,”” who, as one of the churchwardenr, was over-

seeing and carrying out improvements in the grounds. After some
reference to the tombstoms, we got upon the subject of County Wick-
low printing, and he informed me that his fa ther, Mr. William McPhail
(the fcunder of the “ Wicklow 1 News-Letter,” in 1857) had been asked
by the late Mr. Daniel Tighe, of Rosanua, if he would purchage a print-
ing prests of his. Mr. W. McPhail and his elder brother Henry were
separating, it seems, at the time, as regards the printing business.
The former went out in due course the next day, and though his
brother, having heard of it, had anticipated his visit in the morning
with an offer of his own, carried out the purchase, ag I was told. This

appears to have been in the early ‘‘forties.” I see from my copy of

Burke’s *“ Landed CGentry,” of 1863, that Mr. Daniel Tighe was a
nophew of Henry Tighe, M.P. (died 1836), the husband of the authoress
of ‘' Psydhe.”” Daniel married in 1825, and was still living, it seems,
in 1863. |

I saw framed 1 the * News-Letter 7’ office some pages of Wicklow
printing of the first half of last century, and may give subsequently &
few facts as to the pomewhat late and infrequent printing of this town.

With regpeot to the note on the Rosanna press in the Au gust num-
ber of the I.B.L., Mrs. Hamilton would appear from the above edition

of Burke to have Leen tho sister of Henry Tighe, the hushand of the

aubthoress.
